**Limerence** is the [mental state](/wiki/Mental_state "Mental state") of being madly in love[[1]](#cite_note-:26-1)[[2]](#cite_note-beam-limerence-fisher-2) or intensely infatuated[[3]](#cite_note-Hayes-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:16-4) when reciprocation of the feeling is uncertain. This state is characterized by [intrusive thoughts](/wiki/Intrusive_thought "Intrusive thought") and idealization of the loved one (also called "[crystallization](/wiki/Crystallization_(love) "Crystallization (love)")"), typically with a desire for reciprocation to form a [relationship](/wiki/Intimate_relationship "Intimate relationship"). This is accompanied by feelings of [ecstasy](/wiki/Ecstasy_(emotion) "Ecstasy (emotion)") or [despair](/wiki/Despair "Despair"), depending on whether one's feelings seem to be reciprocated or not.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Research on the [biology of romantic love](/wiki/Biology_of_romantic_love "Biology of romantic love") indicates that the early stage of intense [romantic love](/wiki/Romantic_love "Romantic love") (also called [passionate love](/wiki/Passionate_love "Passionate love")) resembles [addiction](/wiki/Addiction "Addiction"), but academics do not currently agree on how [love addictions](/wiki/Love_addiction "Love addiction") are defined.[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8)

The [psychologist](/wiki/Psychologist "Psychologist") [Dorothy Tennov](/wiki/Dorothy_Tennov "Dorothy Tennov") coined the term "limerence" as an alteration of the word "amorance" without other etymologies.[[9]](#cite_note-observer-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) The concept grew out of her work in the 1960s when she interviewed over 500 people on the topic of love, originally published in her book *Love and Limerence*.[[12]](#cite_note-NYT_1977-12)[[13]](#cite_note-wapo1990-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Hatfield_1988_197-15) According to Tennov, "to be in a state of limerence is to feel what is usually termed 'being in love.'"[[16]](#cite_note-16) She coined the term to disambiguate the state from other less-overwhelming emotions and to avoid the implication that people who don't experience it are incapable of love.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_15-18) Tennov was inspired to study romantic love after encountering people in her post as a professor who experienced severe [heartbreak](/wiki/Heartbreak "Heartbreak") and personal perils.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[12]](#cite_note-NYT_1977-12) Tennov's research suggested to her that limerence is normal (although illogical), and a 2025 survey suggested that as many as 50–60% of the population had experienced it.[[20]](#cite_note-:40-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-:41-22)

Limerence is a descriptive concept, rather than a [diagnosis](/wiki/Diagnosis "Diagnosis") or [disorder](/wiki/Mental_disorder "Mental disorder"); it is not in the [DSM](/wiki/Diagnostic_and_Statistical_Manual_of_Mental_Disorders "Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders").[[23]](#cite_note-:43-23) The [polysemous](/wiki/Polysemy "Polysemy") nature of [love](/wiki/Love "Love") words has led to semantic confusion which Tennov meant to clarify, although there is even still disagreement on how "limerence" is defined.[[24]](#cite_note-4th-dim-24)[[18]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_15-18)[[25]](#cite_note-bellamy-stalking-25) Love research has never adopted a unified terminology or definitions.[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[24]](#cite_note-4th-dim-24) According to Tennov and others, limerence can be considered intense romantic love,[[26]](#cite_note-:2-26)[[9]](#cite_note-observer-9)[[27]](#cite_note-usatoday-27) [falling in love](/wiki/Falling_in_love "Falling in love"),[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-diamond2003-30) love madness,[[1]](#cite_note-:26-1)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[2]](#cite_note-beam-limerence-fisher-2) intense [infatuation](/wiki/Infatuation "Infatuation"),[[4]](#cite_note-:16-4)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[30]](#cite_note-diamond2003-30) passionate love with obsessive elements[[15]](#cite_note-Hatfield_1988_197-15)[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[33]](#cite_note-acevedo2009-33) or [lovesickness](/wiki/Lovesickness "Lovesickness").[[13]](#cite_note-wapo1990-13)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-ethnopharma-35) Limerence and [obsessive love](/wiki/Obsessive_love "Obsessive love") are similar, but obsessive love has connotations of possessive and self-defeating behavior.[[36]](#cite_note-:210-36)[[25]](#cite_note-bellamy-stalking-25)[[37]](#cite_note-37) Limerence is also sometimes compared to and contrasted with a [crush](/wiki/Crush_(love) "Crush (love)"), with limerence being much more intense and impacting day-to-day functioning more: "when a crush has taken over your life".[[38]](#cite_note-mccracken-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

*Love and Limerence* has been called the seminal work on romantic love, with Tennov's survey results and the various personal accounts recounted in the book largely marking the start of data collection on the phenomenon.[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

[Dorothy Tennov](/wiki/Dorothy_Tennov "Dorothy Tennov")'s research was intended to be a [scientific](/wiki/Scientific "Scientific") attempt at understanding the nature of [romantic love](/wiki/Romantic_love "Romantic love").[[42]](#cite_note-42) She identified a suite of [psychological](/wiki/Psychology "Psychology") properties associated with a state she called *limerence*—usually termed "being in love", but distinguishable from other types of [attraction](/wiki/Interpersonal_attraction "Interpersonal attraction") patterns that the phrase "in love" might also refer to.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-fisher1998-44)[[2]](#cite_note-beam-limerence-fisher-2)[[45]](#cite_note-45) Other authors have considered limerence to be an [emotional](/wiki/Emotion "Emotion") and [motivational](/wiki/Motivation "Motivation") state for focusing [attention](/wiki/Attention "Attention") on a preferred [mating](/wiki/Mating "Mating") partner[[44]](#cite_note-fisher1998-44)[[46]](#cite_note-fisher2002-46) or an [attachment](/wiki/Attachment_theory "Attachment theory") process.[[47]](#cite_note-hazanshaver-47)[[48]](#cite_note-feeneynoller-48)

[Joe Beam](/wiki/Joe_Beam "Joe Beam") calls limerence the feeling of being "madly in love".[[49]](#cite_note-:13-49)[[2]](#cite_note-beam-limerence-fisher-2) [Nicky Hayes](/wiki/Nicky_Hayes "Nicky Hayes") describes it as "a kind of infatuated, all-absorbing passion", the type of love [Dante](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri") felt towards [Beatrice](/wiki/Beatrice_Portinari "Beatrice Portinari") or that of [Romeo and Juliet](/wiki/Romeo_and_Juliet "Romeo and Juliet").[[3]](#cite_note-Hayes-3) An unfulfilled, intense longing defines the state, where the individual becomes "more or less obsessed by that person and spends much of their time fantasising about them". Hayes suggests it is "the unobtainable nature of the goal which makes the feeling so powerful", and occasional, [intermittent reinforcement](/wiki/Intermittent_reinforcement "Intermittent reinforcement") may be required to support the underlying feelings.[[3]](#cite_note-Hayes-3) [Arthur](/wiki/Arthur_Aron "Arthur Aron") and [Elaine Aron](/wiki/Elaine_Aron "Elaine Aron") noted a "constant, overwhelming, and even debilitating absorption in the unrequited desire" in their characterization of limerence.[[26]](#cite_note-:2-26) [Stanton Peele](/wiki/Stanton_Peele "Stanton Peele") compares it to "severe emotional disability", with an often inappropriate love object.[[50]](#cite_note-peele-limerence-50) [Frank Tallis](/wiki/Frank_Tallis "Frank Tallis") calls it "love that does not need liking—love that may even thrive in response to rejection or contempt" and notes the "striking similarities" with [addiction](/wiki/Addiction "Addiction").[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-tallis-addict-52)

A central feature of limerence, for Tennov, was that her participants really saw the personal flaws of the object of their affection but overlooked them or found them attractive.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-Fisher_2016_21-54) Tennov calls this "[crystallization](/wiki/Crystallization_(love) "Crystallization (love)")", after a description by the French writer [Stendhal](/wiki/Stendhal "Stendhal"). This "crystallized" object of passionate desire is what Tennov calls a "limerent object" (LO), "because to the degree that your reaction to a person is limerent, you respond to *your construction* of LO's qualities".[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Limerence has psychological properties akin to the concept of [passionate love](/wiki/Passionate_love "Passionate love"),[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-:4-57)[[46]](#cite_note-fisher2002-46) but in Tennov's conception, limerence begins before an [intimate relationship](/wiki/Intimate_relationship "Intimate relationship") and before the person experiencing it knows for certain whether it is reciprocated.[[58]](#cite_note-:33-58)[[59]](#cite_note-conceptions-of-limerence-59) Limerence is frequently [unrequited](/wiki/Unrequited_love "Unrequited love") and turns into a [lovesickness](/wiki/Lovesickness "Lovesickness") that can be difficult to escape.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[3]](#cite_note-Hayes-3)[[61]](#cite_note-:31-61)[[62]](#cite_note-money-lovesick-62) Tennov argues that some situational uncertainty is required for the mental preoccupation and feelings to intensify, for example: mixed messages, physical or social obstacles, or even an LO's unsuitability as a partner.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

Not everyone experiences limerence.[[64]](#cite_note-:15-64) Tennov estimated that 50% of women and 35% of men experience limerence based on answers to certain survey questions she administered.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Another survey administered by [neuroscientist](/wiki/Neuroscientist "Neuroscientist") and limerence blogger Tom Bellamy indicated that 64% had experienced it at least once, and 32% "found it so distressing that it was hard to enjoy life".[[22]](#cite_note-:41-22)

It can be difficult for people who have not experienced limerence to understand it, and it is often derided and dismissed as some pathology, or an invention of romantic fiction.[[66]](#cite_note-66) According to Tennov, limerence is not a [mental illness](/wiki/Mental_illness "Mental illness"), although it can be "highly disruptive and extremely painful", called "irrational, silly, embarrassing, and abnormal" or sometimes "the greatest happiness" depending on who is asked.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

## Components

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Components")]

For a similar component listing by Hatfield & Sprecher, see [Passionate Love Scale](/wiki/Passionate_Love_Scale "Passionate Love Scale").

The original components of limerence were:[[68]](#cite_note-68)

> * intrusive thinking about the object of your passionate desire (the limerent object or "LO"), who is a possible sexual partner
> * acute longing for reciprocation
> * dependency of mood on LO's actions or, more accurately, your interpretation of LO's actions with respect to the probability of reciprocation
> * inability to react *limerently* to more than one person at a time (exceptions occur only when limerence is at low ebb—early on or in the last fading)
> * some fleeting and transient relief from unrequited limerent passion through vivid imagination of action by LO that means reciprocation
> * fear of rejection and sometimes incapacitating but always unsettling shyness in LO's presence, especially in the beginning and whenever uncertainty strikes
> * intensification through adversity (at least, up to a point)
> * acute sensitivity to any act or thought or condition that can be interpreted favorably, and an extraordinary ability to devise or invent "reasonable" explanations for why the neutrality that the disinterested observer might see is in fact a sign of hidden passion in the LO
> * an aching of the "heart" (a region in the center front of the chest) when uncertainty is strong
> * buoyancy (a feeling of walking on air) when reciprocation seems evident
> * a general intensity of feeling that leaves other concerns in the background
> * a remarkable ability to emphasize what is truly admirable in LO and to avoid dwelling on the negative, even to respond with a compassion for the negative and render it, emotionally if not perceptually, into another positive attribute.
>
>  — [Dorothy Tennov](/wiki/Dorothy_Tennov "Dorothy Tennov"), *Love and Limerence: The Experience of Being in Love*

## Relation to other concepts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Relation to other concepts")]

### Love

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Love")]

[Dorothy Tennov](/wiki/Dorothy_Tennov "Dorothy Tennov") gives several reasons for inventing a term for the state denoted by limerence (usually termed "being in love").[[86]](#cite_note-86) One principle reason is to resolve ambiguities with the word "[love](/wiki/Love "Love")" being used both to refer to an *act* (which is chosen), as well as to a *state* (which is endured):[[18]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_15-18)

> Many writers on love have complained about semantic difficulties. The dictionary lists two dozen different meanings of the word "love". And how does one distinguish between love and affection, liking, fondness, caring, concern, infatuation, attraction, or desire? [...] Acknowledgment of a distinction between love as a verb, as an action taken by the individual, and love as a state is awkward. Never having fallen in love is not at all a matter of not loving, if loving is defined as caring. Furthermore, this state of "being in love" included feelings that do not properly fit with love defined as concern.

(The type of love that focuses on caring for others is called [compassionate love](/wiki/Compassionate_love "Compassionate love") or [agape](/wiki/Agape "Agape").)[[24]](#cite_note-4th-dim-24)

The other principle reason given is that she encountered people who do not experience limerence. The first such person Tennov discovered was a long-time friend, Helen Payne, whose unfamiliarity with the state emerged during a conversation on an airplane flight together.[[64]](#cite_note-:15-64) Tennov writes that "describing the intricacies of romantic attachments" to Helen was "like trying to describe the color red to one blind from birth".[[87]](#cite_note-87) A person not currently experiencing limerence is called "nonlimerent", but Tennov cautions that it seemed to her that there is no "nonlimerent personality" and that potentially anyone could experience limerence.[[88]](#cite_note-88) Tennov says:[[18]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_15-18)

> I adopted the view that never being in this state was neither more nor less pathological than experiencing it. I wanted to be able to speak about this reliably identifiable condition without giving love's advocates the feeling something precious was being destroyed. Even more important, if using the term "love" denoted the presence of the state, there was the danger that absence of the state would receive negative connotations.

Tennov addresses the issue of whether limerence is love in other passages.[[89]](#cite_note-89) In one passage she clearly says that limerence is love, at least in certain cases:[[90]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_120-90)

> In fully developed limerence, you feel *additionally* what is, in other contexts as well, called love—an extreme degree of feeling that you want LO to be safe, cared for, happy, and all those other positive and noble feelings [...]. That's probably why limerence is called love in all languages. [...] Surely limerence is love at its highest and most glorious peak.

However, Tennov switches in tone and continues on with a fairly negative story of the pain felt by a woman reminiscing over the time she wasted pining for a man she now feels nothing towards, something which occupied her in a time when her father was still alive and her children "were adorable babies who needed their mother's attention." Tennov says this is why we distinguish limerence (this "love") from other loves.[[90]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_120-90) In another passage, Tennov says that while affection and fondness do not demand anything in return, the return of feelings desired in the limerent state means that "Other aspects of your life, including love, are sacrificed in behalf of the all-consuming need." and that "While limerence has been called love, it is not love."[[91]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_71-91)

### Romantic love

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Romantic love")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Rogelio_de_Egusquiza_-_Tristan_and_Isolt_%28Death%29_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Rogelio_de_Egusquiza_-_Tristan_and_Isolt_%28Death%29_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rogelio_de_Egusquiza_-_Tristan_and_Isolt_(Death)_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*[Tristan and Isolde (Death)](/wiki/Tristan_and_Isolde_(Egusquiza) "Tristan and Isolde (Egusquiza)")*, by [Rogelio de Egusquiza](/wiki/Rogelio_de_Egusquiza "Rogelio de Egusquiza"). In this myth, the two drink a [love potion](/wiki/Love_potion "Love potion") by mistake, when Iseult is due to be married to Tristan's uncle, a king. In one version of the story, Tristan dies of a [broken heart](/wiki/Broken_heart "Broken heart") after a signal sent by Iseult is miscommunicated to him as a rejection.[[92]](#cite_note-92) In another version, Tristan is mortally wounded but Iseult cannot save him and gives up her spirit. The story is said to be the quintissential courtly romance.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

For romantic love as a motivational state (a different definition, used in the sciences), see [Biology of romantic love § Definition of romantic love](/wiki/Biology_of_romantic_love#Definition_of_romantic_love "Biology of romantic love").

[Dorothy Tennov](/wiki/Dorothy_Tennov "Dorothy Tennov") sometimes considers limerence to be synonymous with "[romantic love](/wiki/Romantic_love "Romantic love")",[[94]](#cite_note-:37-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[9]](#cite_note-observer-9) a term with a complicated history and definition. A cultural ancestor to Tennov's concept was the literary tradition of romantic love, involving often tragic or unfulfilled love, or early depictions of limerence.[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[94]](#cite_note-:37-94)[[3]](#cite_note-Hayes-3) Literature and poetry provided early self-reports of the kind of experience Tennov was interested in studying.[[97]](#cite_note-97) Some examples of romantic love stories in this vein are *[Layla and Majnun](/wiki/Layla_and_Majnun "Layla and Majnun")*, *[Tristan and Iseult](/wiki/Tristan_and_Iseult "Tristan and Iseult")*, [Dante](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri") and [Beatrice](/wiki/Beatrice_Portinari "Beatrice Portinari") (from *[La Vita Nuova](/wiki/La_Vita_Nuova "La Vita Nuova")*), *[Romeo and Juliet](/wiki/Romeo_and_Juliet "Romeo and Juliet")* and *[The Sorrows of Young Werther](/wiki/The_Sorrows_of_Young_Werther "The Sorrows of Young Werther")*.[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[3]](#cite_note-Hayes-3) [Anakin](/wiki/Anakin_Skywalker "Anakin Skywalker") and [Padmé](/wiki/Padm%C3%A9_Amidala "Padmé Amidala") from *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* are a modern depiction.[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101)

The literary genre dates back to [troubadour](/wiki/Troubadour "Troubadour") poetry from the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") (or earlier), also known as "[courtly love](/wiki/Courtly_love "Courtly love")".[[102]](#cite_note-102) Tennov credits [Andreas Capellanus](/wiki/Andreas_Capellanus "Andreas Capellanus") as describing limerence "very accurately" in [*The Art of Courtly Love*](/wiki/De_amore_(Andreas_Capellanus) "De amore (Andreas Capellanus)"), a book of statutes for the "proper" conduct of lovers.[[103]](#cite_note-:29-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104) The work includes rules such as "A true lover is constantly and without intermission possessed by the thoughts of his beloved." and "The easy attainment of love makes it of little value; difficulty of attainment makes it prized."[[105]](#cite_note-Tallis_2004_96-105) The work is believed to have helped spread romantic love culture throughout [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe").[[105]](#cite_note-Tallis_2004_96-105)[[106]](#cite_note-Lee_1998_54–55-106) Romantic love in this sense is sometimes held to be [socially constructed](/wiki/Social_construct "Social construct") (often by critics), but Tennov argues that limerence has a biological basis.[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108)

Tennov sometimes considers limerence synonymous with "[falling in love](/wiki/Falling_in_love "Falling in love")",[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-:38-110) a concept which also has origins in the romantic tradition and the idea that love is tragic—evoking a connotation of physically falling over or losing consciousness.[[110]](#cite_note-:38-110)

"Romantic love" originally referenced this courtly idea, but then came to have other connotations.[[111]](#cite_note-111) In modern scientific literature, "romantic love" is instead often used as a synonym for "[passionate love](/wiki/Passionate_and_companionate_love "Passionate and companionate love")", a more general concept, also often associated with limerence.[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-:52-113) In her era, Tennov called the scientific literature "confused and contradictory".[[114]](#cite_note-114) [John Alan Lee](/wiki/John_Alan_Lee "John Alan Lee") has also complained about this reduction to a monolithic typology, or "one true love".[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116) [Helen Fisher](/wiki/Helen_Fisher_(anthropologist) "Helen Fisher (anthropologist)") commented that she preferred the term "romantic love" for its meaning in society.[[117]](#cite_note-madlyinlove-117)

### Passionate and companionate love

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Passionate and companionate love")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Frederick_Goodall_RA_-_Passionate_encounter.jpg/250px-Frederick_Goodall_RA_-_Passionate_encounter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frederick_Goodall_RA_-_Passionate_encounter.jpg)

*Passionate Encounter*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/La_Promenade%2C_by_Pierre-Auguste_Renoir.jpg/250px-La_Promenade%2C_by_Pierre-Auguste_Renoir.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_Promenade,_by_Pierre-Auguste_Renoir.jpg)

*La Promenade*

Main article: [Passionate and companionate love](/wiki/Passionate_and_companionate_love "Passionate and companionate love")

Limerence is often associated with "[passionate love](/wiki/Passionate_love "Passionate love")", with [Elaine Hatfield](/wiki/Elaine_Hatfield "Elaine Hatfield") considering them synonymous, and commenting in 2016 that they're "much the same".[[15]](#cite_note-Hatfield_1988_197-15)[[118]](#cite_note-potentgrip-118)[[113]](#cite_note-:52-113) Many researchers have considered them synonymous.[[30]](#cite_note-diamond2003-30)[[46]](#cite_note-fisher2002-46)[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[33]](#cite_note-acevedo2009-33) Passionate love is:[[119]](#cite_note-119)

> A state of intense longing for union with an other. Reciprocated love (union with the other) is associated with fulfillment and ecstasy. Unrequited love (separation) with emptiness; with anxiety, or despair. A state of profound physiological arousal.

Passionate love is linked to *[passion](/wiki/Passion_(emotion) "Passion (emotion)")*, as in intense [emotion](/wiki/Emotion "Emotion"): for example, [joy](/wiki/Joy "Joy") and [fulfillment](/wiki/Self-fulfillment "Self-fulfillment"), but also [anguish](/wiki/Anguish "Anguish") and [agony](/wiki/Suffering "Suffering").[[120]](#cite_note-Hatfield_1985_58-120) According to Hatfield, passion is a "hodgepodge of conflicting emotions", and the original meaning "*was* agony—as in [Christ's passion](/wiki/Passion_of_Jesus "Passion of Jesus")."[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[120]](#cite_note-Hatfield_1985_58-120) Passionate love is contrasted with the less intense "[companionate love](/wiki/Companionate_love "Companionate love")": "the [affection](/wiki/Affection "Affection") we feel for those with whom our lives are deeply entwined".[[122]](#cite_note-122)[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)

In *Love and Limerence*, [Dorothy Tennov](/wiki/Dorothy_Tennov "Dorothy Tennov") also lists passionate love among her synonyms for limerence, and refers to Hatfield's early writings on the concept.[[123]](#cite_note-123) Tennov's study, however, focused on the aspects of love which cause distress, and on individuals over relationships.[[124]](#cite_note-124)[[125]](#cite_note-:32-125) Another problem she encountered in her research was that informants would use terms like "passionate love", "romantic love" and "being in love" to refer to mental states other than what she refers to as limerence.[[126]](#cite_note-126) Informants would use the word "obsession", yet not report the [intrusive thoughts](/wiki/Intrusive_thought "Intrusive thought") necessary to limerence, only that "thoughts of the person are frequent and pleasurable".[[127]](#cite_note-127)

Passionate love is commonly measured with the *[Passionate Love Scale](/wiki/Passionate_Love_Scale "Passionate Love Scale")* (PLS), originally designed to measure the same state denoted by limerence.[[113]](#cite_note-:52-113)[[128]](#cite_note-:25-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129) Later research found the PLS has overly broad questions, and it actually has two general components (called [factors](/wiki/Factor_analysis "Factor analysis")): an *obsession* factor and a *non*-obsession factor.[[130]](#cite_note-ias-130)[[33]](#cite_note-acevedo2009-33) The PLS obsession factor has items like "Sometimes I feel I can't control my thoughts; they are obsessively on my partner." and "An existence without my partner would be dark and dismal."[[33]](#cite_note-acevedo2009-33)[[131]](#cite_note-:42-131) Limerence is comparable to passionate love with obsession:[[33]](#cite_note-acevedo2009-33)

> Passionate love, "a state of intense longing for union with another" [...], also referred to as [...] "limerence" (Tennov, 1979), includes an obsessive element, characterized by intrusive thinking, uncertainty, and mood swings.

The PLS non-obsession factor has items like "For me, my partner is the perfect romantic partner." and "I want my partner—physically, emotionally, and mentally."[[33]](#cite_note-acevedo2009-33) These love feelings (without obsession) can sustain over a longer period, according to newer research.[[132]](#cite_note-nbc2009-132)[[133]](#cite_note-time-marriage-133)[[33]](#cite_note-acevedo2009-33)

### Infatuation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Infatuation")]

"[Infatuation](/wiki/Infatuation "Infatuation")" has been considered synonymous with concepts like [passionate love](/wiki/Passionate_love "Passionate love"), "being in love" and limerence,[[30]](#cite_note-diamond2003-30)[[46]](#cite_note-fisher2002-46)[[33]](#cite_note-acevedo2009-33) but limerence is supposed to be more intense than a simple infatuation.[[2]](#cite_note-beam-limerence-fisher-2) [Dorothy Tennov](/wiki/Dorothy_Tennov "Dorothy Tennov") has stated that she did not use the word "infatuation" because while there is overlap, the word evokes different connotations.[[125]](#cite_note-:32-125) In one type of distinction, people use "infatuation" to express disapproval or to refer to unsatisfactory relationships, and "love" to refer to satisfactory ones.[[134]](#cite_note-134) In *Love and Limerence*, Tennov considers "infatuation" to be [pejorative](/wiki/Pejorative "Pejorative"), for example, being used to label [teenage](/wiki/Teenage "Teenage") fantasizing about a [celebrity](/wiki/Celebrity "Celebrity") which is actually limerence.[[135]](#cite_note-135)

In the [triangular theory of love](/wiki/Triangular_theory_of_love "Triangular theory of love"), by [Robert Sternberg](/wiki/Robert_Sternberg "Robert Sternberg"), "infatuation" refers to romantic passion without intimacy (or closeness) and without commitment, which he has stated is essentially the same as limerence.[[136]](#cite_note-:0-136)[[137]](#cite_note-Tallis_2004_45-137)

### Independent emotion systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Independent emotion systems")]

[Helen Fisher's](/wiki/Helen_Fisher_(anthropologist) "Helen Fisher (anthropologist)") popular theory of [independent emotion systems](/wiki/Biology_of_romantic_love#Independent_emotion_systems "Biology of romantic love") posits that there are three primary systems involved with human [reproduction](/wiki/Reproduction "Reproduction"), [mating](/wiki/Mating "Mating") and [parenting](/wiki/Parenting "Parenting"): *lust* (the sex drive, or sexual desire), *attraction* (passionate love, infatuation or limerence) and *attachment* (companionate love). These three systems regularly work in concert together but serve different purposes and can also work independently.[[44]](#cite_note-fisher1998-44)[[46]](#cite_note-fisher2002-46)[[138]](#cite_note-co-opted-138) According to Fisher, lust, attraction and attachment can occur in any order.[[139]](#cite_note-139)[[140]](#cite_note-140) Independent emotions theory has been critiqued as being oversimplified, but the general idea of separate systems remains useful.[[138]](#cite_note-co-opted-138)

When limerence is a component in an [affair](/wiki/Affair "Affair"), for example, Fisher's theory can be used to help explain this.[[46]](#cite_note-fisher2002-46)[[141]](#cite_note-141) Fisher's theory is that a person can feel deep attachment for a long-term spouse, *while* they're in limerence with somebody else, *while* they can be sexually attracted to still yet other people.[[46]](#cite_note-fisher2002-46)[[142]](#cite_note-142) [Joe Beam](/wiki/Joe_Beam "Joe Beam") comments that if somebody in a committed relationship ends up in limerence like this, it will pull them out of their relationship.[[49]](#cite_note-:13-49)

Fisher's theory has also been used to explain "[platonic](/wiki/Platonic_love "Platonic love")" limerence (without [sexual desire](/wiki/Sexual_desire "Sexual desire")), because romantic love and sexual desire are functionally distinct.[[30]](#cite_note-diamond2003-30)[[143]](#cite_note-143) Tennov encountered this occasionally in her own research, finding cases of otherwise [heterosexual](/wiki/Heterosexuality "Heterosexuality") women experiencing limerence for an older woman (compared to "hero worship"), but dismissed it as being outside her theory.[[144]](#cite_note-144) [Lisa Diamond](/wiki/Lisa_M._Diamond "Lisa M. Diamond") argues this is possible (even in contradiction to [sexual orientation](/wiki/Sexual_orientation "Sexual orientation")) because the brain systems evolved by [repurposing the systems for mother-infant bonding](/wiki/Biology_of_romantic_love#Co-option_of_mother-infant_bonding "Biology of romantic love") (a process called [exaptation](/wiki/Exaptation "Exaptation")). According to this theory, it would not have been adaptive for a parent to only be able to bond with an opposite sex child, so the systems must have evolved independent of sexual orientation.[[30]](#cite_note-diamond2003-30)

### Attachment theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Attachment theory")]

[John Bowlby's](/wiki/John_Bowlby "John Bowlby") concept of "[attachment](/wiki/Attachment_theory "Attachment theory")" refers to a system evolved to keep infants in proximity of their caregiver (or "attachment figure").[[30]](#cite_note-diamond2003-30)[[47]](#cite_note-hazanshaver-47)[[24]](#cite_note-4th-dim-24) An attachment figure is a "secure base" for safety while exploring the environment, the child seeks proximity with the attachment figure when threatened, and suffers distress when separated.[[24]](#cite_note-4th-dim-24)[[47]](#cite_note-hazanshaver-47) A prominent theory suggests this system is reused for adult [pair bonds](/wiki/Pair_bond "Pair bond"), as an [exaptation](/wiki/Exaptation "Exaptation") or co-option, whereby a given trait takes on a new purpose.[[47]](#cite_note-hazanshaver-47)[[138]](#cite_note-co-opted-138)[[30]](#cite_note-diamond2003-30) "Attachment style" refers to differences in thoughts and behaviors, relating to the concept of security vs. insecurity.[[145]](#cite_note-fraley-shaver-style-145)[[47]](#cite_note-hazanshaver-47) This is split into components of anxiety (worrying the partner is available, attentive and responsive) and avoidance (preference not to rely on others or open up emotionally).[[145]](#cite_note-fraley-shaver-style-145)

In [Helen Fisher's](/wiki/Helen_Fisher_(anthropologist) "Helen Fisher (anthropologist)") theory, limerence and attachment are considered different systems with different purposes.[[44]](#cite_note-fisher1998-44)[[46]](#cite_note-fisher2002-46) In the past, it has also been suggested that limerence could be related to the anxious attachment style.[[47]](#cite_note-hazanshaver-47)[[48]](#cite_note-feeneynoller-48) However, in their original 1987 paper about this, [Cindy Hazan](/wiki/Cindy_Hazan "Cindy Hazan") and [Phillip Shaver](/wiki/Phillip_Shaver "Phillip Shaver") caution they aren't implying the early phase of [romance](/wiki/Romance "Romance") is equivalent to being attached.[[47]](#cite_note-hazanshaver-47) Other prominent authors have also argued that attachment theory cannot replace concepts like love styles or types of love.[[146]](#cite_note-146)[[24]](#cite_note-4th-dim-24) Limerence is considered a unique state which is distinct from attachment style, although people who have an anxious attachment style are more likely to have experienced it according to one survey.[[147]](#cite_note-:36-147)

A 1990 study found considerable overlap of distributions between all three attachment styles and limerence (reported at similar frequencies), but the 15% of participants with an anxious attachment style scored about 10–20% higher on obsessive preoccupation and emotional dependence, and avoidants idealized more.[[48]](#cite_note-feeneynoller-48)[[148]](#cite_note-148) Along with scoring highly on limerence, the anxious group also scored highly on the [agape](/wiki/Agape "Agape") love attitude, for selfless, all-giving love.[[48]](#cite_note-feeneynoller-48)

### Love styles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Love styles")]

The concept of a "[love style](/wiki/Colour_wheel_theory_of_love "Colour wheel theory of love")" was invented by the [sociologist](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology") [John Alan Lee](/wiki/John_Alan_Lee "John Alan Lee"), to distinguish between different ways to love, or different types of love stories.[[149]](#cite_note-lee-typology-149)[[150]](#cite_note-150) Limerence is considered similar or related to the love style [mania](/wiki/Colour_wheel_theory_of_love#Mania "Colour wheel theory of love") (or manic love), named after the [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") *theia mania* (the madness from the gods).[[151]](#cite_note-:6-151)[[48]](#cite_note-feeneynoller-48)[[26]](#cite_note-:2-26)[[152]](#cite_note-152) Lee developed his mania concept from sources similar to Tennov, like [Andreas Capellanus](/wiki/Andreas_Capellanus "Andreas Capellanus") and [courtly love](/wiki/Courtly_love "Courtly love").[[153]](#cite_note-153)[[154]](#cite_note-154)[[106]](#cite_note-Lee_1998_54–55-106) Both Lee and Tennov refer to "love madness", and Peele & Brodsky's *Love and Addiction*.[[155]](#cite_note-155)[[1]](#cite_note-:26-1)[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_x-157)

A manic lover is obsessively preoccupied with the beloved.[[158]](#cite_note-Lee_1988_45-158) When asked to recall their childhood, a typical manic lover recalls it as unhappy, and they're usually lonely, dissatisfied adults.[[159]](#cite_note-159) They're anxious to fall in love, but they're unsure of which physical type they prefer.[[149]](#cite_note-lee-typology-149) Because they're unsure of who to fall in love with, they often fall in love with somebody quite inappropriate (a stranger, or even somebody they initially dislike) and project onto them the qualities they want but don't actually have.[[160]](#cite_note-160)[[161]](#cite_note-Lee_1988_46-161) According to Lee, "Mania can become almost an addiction nearly impossible for the addict to end on his own initiative."[[162]](#cite_note-162) Mania is often the first love style of a young person, but others may not experience it until middle age—for example, after a [marriage](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") has lost interest.[[161]](#cite_note-Lee_1988_46-161) According to Lee, a cycle of manic loves is often caused by a desperate need to be in love, the cause of which the manic lover must locate and remedy to break free.[[163]](#cite_note-163)

Lee describes the manic lover as [jealous](/wiki/Jealousy "Jealousy"),[[158]](#cite_note-Lee_1988_45-158) but Tennov believes that a person can be limerent and not be jealous.[[164]](#cite_note-164)

Among the other love styles, mania can be closely compared to [eros](/wiki/Colour_wheel_theory_of_love#Eros "Colour wheel theory of love") ([erotic love](/wiki/Eros_(concept) "Eros (concept)"), or love of beauty).[[165]](#cite_note-:35-165)[[166]](#cite_note-166) Both are often considered "[romantic love](/wiki/Romantic_love "Romantic love")", both involve "[falling in love](/wiki/Falling_in_love "Falling in love")", and taken together they correspond to the way the *[Passionate Love Scale](/wiki/Passionate_Love_Scale "Passionate Love Scale")* is defined.[[165]](#cite_note-:35-165)[[167]](#cite_note-167)[[33]](#cite_note-acevedo2009-33) An erotic lover is *also* intensely preoccupied with their beloved, but the thoughts are optimistic, while a manic lover is insecure.[[168]](#cite_note-168) *Unlike* a manic lover, however, the erotic lover is aware of a physical type they consider ideal.[[169]](#cite_note-169) As such, eros begins with a powerful initial attraction, referred to by [Stendhal](/wiki/Stendhal "Stendhal") as "a sudden sensation of recognition and hope".[[170]](#cite_note-170) The eros love style is not "blind", then.[[171]](#cite_note-171) According to Lee, only manic lovers typically "[crystallize](/wiki/Crystallization_(love) "Crystallization (love)")" (as Stendhal described it) and ignore shortcomings and flaws in their beloved.[[172]](#cite_note-172) The erotic lover also recalls their childhood as happy, and eros has been associated with [secure attachment](/wiki/Secure_attachment "Secure attachment"), while mania has been associated with [attachment anxiety](/wiki/Anxious-preoccupied_attachment "Anxious-preoccupied attachment") and [neuroticism](/wiki/Neuroticism "Neuroticism").[[173]](#cite_note-173)[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-175) A third style, manic eros, is a mixture "moving either toward a more stable eros or toward full-blown mania". Some are erotic lovers under a temporary strain (moving toward mania), while others are manic lovers with a self-confident and helping partner (moving toward eros).[[176]](#cite_note-176)

According to Lee, the love style [ludus](/wiki/Colour_wheel_theory_of_love#Ludus "Colour wheel theory of love") (noncommittal love as a game, avoidance and juggling multiple partners, e.g. [Don Juan](/wiki/Don_Juan "Don Juan")) and mania possess a "fatal attraction" for one another. It's surprisingly common, but not a good match for happy, mutual love.[[177]](#cite_note-177) According to Tennov, Don Juan was probably nonlimerent, "more interested in exploiting the feeling in others for his own sexual gratification", although nonlimerence doesn't necessitate this.[[12]](#cite_note-NYT_1977-12)[[178]](#cite_note-178)

### Love addiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Love addiction")]

"[Love addiction](/wiki/Love_addiction "Love addiction")" is a [heterogeneous](/wiki/Heterogeneous "Heterogeneous") construct under discussion as a potential [mental disorder](/wiki/Mental_disorder "Mental disorder"), but does not yet exist in any [psychiatric](/wiki/Psychiatry "Psychiatry") [nosology](/wiki/Nosology "Nosology") (e.g. not in the [DSM](/wiki/Diagnostic_and_Statistical_Manual_of_Mental_Disorders "Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders")).[[179]](#cite_note-cavalli-2025-179)[[180]](#cite_note-:14-180)[[181]](#cite_note-181) Academics do not currently agree on when love is an addiction or when it needs to be treated. In a narrow view, love could be considered addiction only when it involves abnormal processes carrying negative consequences; alternatively, a broader view is that all love might be addiction, or simply an [appetite](/wiki/Appetite "Appetite"), similar to how humans are dependent on [food](/wiki/Food "Food").[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8) Authors such as [Helen Fisher](/wiki/Helen_Fisher_(anthropologist) "Helen Fisher (anthropologist)") have also included those "who have been rejected or broken up with" as love addicts.[[182]](#cite_note-182)[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7)

Limerence has been included in this discussion, and likened to a love addiction.[[50]](#cite_note-peele-limerence-50)[[183]](#cite_note-183)[[184]](#cite_note-:18-184) [Stanton Peele](/wiki/Stanton_Peele "Stanton Peele"), a pioneering author on love addiction,[[180]](#cite_note-:14-180) has commented on limerence, calling it a "clinical condition" which "leads people (primarily women) desperately to pursue often inappropriate love objects, frequently to fail at relationships, and to be incapable of learning from such experiences".[[50]](#cite_note-peele-limerence-50) [Sharon Brehm](/wiki/Sharon_Brehm "Sharon Brehm") has wondered what limerence[[185]](#cite_note-185) would feel like "if there were a cultural tradition encouraging us to work with it rather than be assailed by it".[[186]](#cite_note-186)

### Erotomania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Erotomania")]

Limerence is different from [erotomania](/wiki/Erotomania "Erotomania"), a [delusional disorder](/wiki/Delusional_disorder "Delusional disorder") where the sufferer falsely believes their love is secretly reciprocated when it isn't, and invents ways to interpret outright rejections as unserious.[[23]](#cite_note-:43-23)[[187]](#cite_note-tallis-erotomania-187) A person in limerence by comparison might "grasp for hope" and misinterpret signals, or [imagine](/wiki/Imagination "Imagination") reciprocation in a [fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy_(psychology) "Fantasy (psychology)"), but they will understand a rejection.[[188]](#cite_note-188) [Helen Fisher](/wiki/Helen_Fisher_(anthropologist) "Helen Fisher (anthropologist)") and colleagues have stated that erotomania may be a type of [schizophrenia](/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia"), and may not involve the same brain [reward system](/wiki/Reward_system "Reward system") activity as [romantic love](/wiki/Romantic_love "Romantic love").[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7)

## Evolutionary purpose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Evolutionary purpose")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Peacock_Plumage.jpg/250px-Peacock_Plumage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peacock_Plumage.jpg)

Limerence might have evolved as a [handicap signal](/wiki/Handicap_principle "Handicap principle"), similar to a [peacock](/wiki/Peacock "Peacock")'s tail, but signaling extreme [commitment](/wiki/Committed_relationship "Committed relationship").[[189]](#cite_note-:232-189)

Further information: [Biology of romantic love § Evolution of systems](/wiki/Biology_of_romantic_love#Evolution_of_systems "Biology of romantic love")

[Dorothy Tennov's](/wiki/Dorothy_Tennov "Dorothy Tennov") speculation was that limerence has an [evolutionary](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution") purpose.[[190]](#cite_note-190)[[191]](#cite_note-Tennov_1998_81–82-191)

> For what ultimate cause might the state of limerence be a proximate cause? In other words, why were people who became limerent successful, maybe more successful than others, in passing their genes on to succeeding generations[.] Did limerence evolve to cement a relationship long enough to get the offspring up and running? [...] The most consistent result of limerence is mating, not merely sexual interaction but also commitment, the establishment of a shared domicile in the form of a cozy nest built for the enjoyment of ecstasy, for reproduction, and for the rearing of children.

According to the evolutionary theory by the [anthropologist](/wiki/Anthropologist "Anthropologist") [Helen Fisher](/wiki/Helen_Fisher_(anthropologist) "Helen Fisher (anthropologist)"), limerence is the activation of a motivation system for choosing and focusing energy on a potential mating partner. This brain system evolved for mammalian [mate choice](/wiki/Mate_choice "Mate choice"), also called "courtship attraction". In this phenomenon, a preferred mating partner is chosen based on a display of physical traits (such as a [peacock](/wiki/Peacock "Peacock")'s tail feathers) or other behaviors.[[44]](#cite_note-fisher1998-44)[[46]](#cite_note-fisher2002-46)[[192]](#cite_note-:8-192) Fisher also includes the attraction to [personality traits](/wiki/Personality_traits "Personality traits") and other characteristics in her mate choice theory for humans.[[193]](#cite_note-193)[[194]](#cite_note-194)[[195]](#cite_note-195) Who a person falls in love with then is determined by their "[love map](/wiki/Lovemap "Lovemap")", a largely unconscious list of traits they desire in an ideal partner. Love maps begin forming during childhood based on experiences with parents and friends, among other associations, but also change over time.[[196]](#cite_note-196)[[197]](#cite_note-197) In most species, courtship attraction is brief, but intense romantic love can last much longer in humans.[[192]](#cite_note-:8-192) A competing evolutionary theory to Fisher's is that courtship attraction only encompasses something like [love at first sight](/wiki/Love_at_first_sight "Love at first sight") attraction, and the obsessive thoughts and intense attraction associated with early-stage romantic love instead evolved by [co-opting](/wiki/Exaptation "Exaptation") (or re-using) the brain systems for mother-infant bonding. In this theory, romantic love may serve the function of mate choice but the brain systems were not originally for this.[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[138]](#cite_note-co-opted-138)[[30]](#cite_note-diamond2003-30)

The [neuroscientist](/wiki/Neuroscientist "Neuroscientist") Tom Bellamy believes that limerence evolved as a form of high-risk "extreme pair bonding" which can be explained as a handicap signal.[[189]](#cite_note-:232-189) The [handicap principle](/wiki/Handicap_principle "Handicap principle") in evolutionary theory is based on a contention between honest and fake [signaling](/wiki/Signalling_theory "Signalling theory"). When real [emotions](/wiki/Emotion "Emotion") evolve, a niche is created for sham emotions (e.g. fake facial expressions) which are less risky to express. One explanation for why honest signals can evolve without becoming worthless (because of competing fakers) is that the honest signal can evolve if it's too expensive to fake. One example in nature is the peacock's tail, an example of [conspicuous consumption](/wiki/Conspicuous_consumption "Conspicuous consumption"), a cumbersome display which consumes nutrients. Only a healthy peacock can afford it, so in that case it may have evolved *because* it was a handicap, and used by females of the species as an indicator of health.[[198]](#cite_note-:152-198) Limerence can be seen as a handicap signal meant to prove one's true commitment to their limerent object. Limerence might have evolved to leave the person experiencing it so insanely besotted that they would not leave for another mate, even a more valuable one.[[189]](#cite_note-:232-189) According to Helen Fisher's theory, [monogamy](/wiki/Monogamy "Monogamy") emerged at a time when mothers needed extra food and protection (when [bipedalism](/wiki/Bipedalism "Bipedalism") evolved, and then infant [altriciality](/wiki/Altriciality "Altriciality") later), so romantic love evolved to last long enough while a mother cares for an infant.[[199]](#cite_note-199)[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7)

Tennov suggested that if the neural "machinery" for limerence is not a universal among all humans, then having both [phenotypes](/wiki/Phenotype "Phenotype") (limerent and nonlimerent) in the population might be beneficial and an [evolutionarily stable strategy](/wiki/Evolutionarily_stable_strategy "Evolutionarily stable strategy").[[200]](#cite_note-200) Limerents and nonlimerents tend not to always get along, nor have compatible relationship interests.[[201]](#cite_note-201) Limerence would also be affected by culture, according to Tennov. A culture which idealizes limerence might cause the nonlimerent LO to be more tolerant (or even imitate it), whereas a culture which is hostile to limerence might cause it to be denied, hidden or suppressed.[[202]](#cite_note-202)

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

Further information: [Biology of romantic love § Mechanics](/wiki/Biology_of_romantic_love#Mechanics "Biology of romantic love"), and [Reward theory of attraction](/wiki/Reward_theory_of_attraction "Reward theory of attraction")

### Addiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Addiction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Mesocorticolimbic_Circuit.png/250px-Mesocorticolimbic_Circuit.png)](/wiki/File:Mesocorticolimbic_Circuit.png)

Key connections in the [mesocorticolimbic pathway](/wiki/Mesocorticolimbic_pathway "Mesocorticolimbic pathway").

Limerence has been called an [addiction](/wiki/Addiction "Addiction").[[157]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_x-157)[[38]](#cite_note-mccracken-38)[[203]](#cite_note-203) The early stage of [romantic love](/wiki/Romantic_love "Romantic love") is being compared to a [behavioral addiction](/wiki/Behavioral_addiction "Behavioral addiction") (i.e. addiction to a non-substance) but the "substance" involved is the loved person.[[52]](#cite_note-tallis-addict-52)[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7)[[204]](#cite_note-:7-204)[[205]](#cite_note-205) A team led by [Helen Fisher](/wiki/Helen_Fisher_(anthropologist) "Helen Fisher (anthropologist)") used [fMRI](/wiki/Functional_magnetic_resonance_imaging "Functional magnetic resonance imaging") to find that people who had "just fallen madly in love" showed activation in an area of the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain") called the [ventral tegmental area](/wiki/Ventral_tegmental_area "Ventral tegmental area") (VTA) while looking at a photograph of their beloved.[[46]](#cite_note-fisher2002-46)[[128]](#cite_note-:25-128)[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7) The VTA is an area in the [midbrain](/wiki/Midbrain "Midbrain") which produces [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") and projects to other [reward system](/wiki/Reward_system "Reward system") areas, like the [nucleus accumbens](/wiki/Nucleus_accumbens "Nucleus accumbens") and [caudate nucleus](/wiki/Caudate_nucleus "Caudate nucleus") which have also been active in brain scans of romantic love.[[128]](#cite_note-:25-128)[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7)[[206]](#cite_note-:11-206)[[207]](#cite_note-:9-207) Dopamine signaling in the VTA is the origin of a phenomenon called [incentive salience](/wiki/Incentive_salience "Incentive salience"), also called "wanting" (in quotes). This is the property by which cues in the environment stand out to a person and become attention-grabbing and attractive, like a "motivational magnet" which pulls a person towards a particular reward.[[208]](#cite_note-:10-208)[[206]](#cite_note-:11-206)[[207]](#cite_note-:9-207) People in love are thought to experience incentive salience in response to their beloved.[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7)

In addiction research, a distinction is drawn between "wanting" a reward (i.e. incentive salience, tied to [mesocorticolimbic](/wiki/Mesocorticolimbic_circuit "Mesocorticolimbic circuit") dopamine) and "liking" a reward (i.e. pleasure, tied to [hedonic hotspots](/wiki/Hedonic_hotspots "Hedonic hotspots")), aspects which are dissociable.[[208]](#cite_note-:10-208)[[206]](#cite_note-:11-206) People can be addicted to drugs and [compulsively](/wiki/Compulsive_behavior "Compulsive behavior") seek them out, even when taking the drug no longer results in a high or the addiction is detrimental to one's life.[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7) They can also "want" (i.e. feel compelled towards, in the sense of incentive salience) something which they do not [cognitively](/wiki/Cognition "Cognition") wish for.[[208]](#cite_note-:10-208) In a similar way, people who are in love may "want" a loved person even when interactions with them are not pleasurable. For example, they may want to contact an ex-partner after a rejection, even when the experience will only be painful.[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7) It is also possible for a person to be "in love" with somebody they do not like, or who treats them poorly.[[209]](#cite_note-Hatfield_1985_103–105-209) Fisher's team proposes that romantic love is a "positive addiction" (i.e. not harmful) when requited and a "negative addiction" when unrequited or inappropriate.[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7)

For a person in limerence that goes unrequited, the pleasurable aspects tend to diminish over time, with the person becoming lovesick and the addiction being maintained more by avoidance of the pain of separation.[[52]](#cite_note-tallis-addict-52)[[61]](#cite_note-:31-61)[[35]](#cite_note-ethnopharma-35)[[62]](#cite_note-money-lovesick-62)

### Lovesickness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Lovesickness")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/The_Love_Potion.jpg/250px-The_Love_Potion.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Love_Potion.jpg)

*[The Love Potion](/wiki/The_Love_Potion "The Love Potion")*, by [Evelyn De Morgan](/wiki/Evelyn_De_Morgan "Evelyn De Morgan"). According to Tennov, "the love potion’s ingredients remain secret. Limerence is unaffected by the intensity of our desire to call it into or out of existence at our wills.",[[210]](#cite_note-210) but she asks "Is the love potion a hormone?"[[211]](#cite_note-211)

Main article: [Lovesickness](/wiki/Lovesickness "Lovesickness")

Usually limerence is [unrequited](/wiki/Unrequited_love "Unrequited love"), and a horrible experience for the limerent person, even debilitating for some.[[212]](#cite_note-thelovedrug-212)[[62]](#cite_note-money-lovesick-62) [Lovesickness](/wiki/Lovesickness "Lovesickness") is the resulting [mental state](/wiki/Mental_state "Mental state"), characterized by [addictive cravings](/wiki/Drug_withdrawal "Drug withdrawal"), [frustration](/wiki/Frustration "Frustration"), [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)"), melancholy and [intrusive thinking](/wiki/Intrusive_thought "Intrusive thought").[[61]](#cite_note-:31-61)[[35]](#cite_note-ethnopharma-35)[[62]](#cite_note-money-lovesick-62) In [Dorothy Tennov's](/wiki/Dorothy_Tennov "Dorothy Tennov") survey group, 42% reported being "severely depressed about a love affair" and 17% said they "often thought of committing [suicide](/wiki/Suicide "Suicide")".[[213]](#cite_note-213) [Helen Fisher's](/wiki/Helen_Fisher_(anthropologist) "Helen Fisher (anthropologist)") [fMRI](/wiki/Functional_magnetic_resonance_imaging "Functional magnetic resonance imaging") scans of rejected lovers showed activation in brain areas associated with physical pain, craving and assessing one's gains and losses.[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7) A limerent person can self-isolate, or be [distracted](/wiki/Distraction "Distraction"), even to the detriment of school or job performance.[[214]](#cite_note-214)[[62]](#cite_note-money-lovesick-62)

A [shyness](/wiki/Shyness "Shyness") and [confusion](/wiki/Confusion "Confusion") manifests out of fear of rejection when an LO is around—sometimes even in those normally confident, with the limerent person "terribly worried that [their] own actions may bring about disaster".[[215]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_49-215) A 28-year-old truck driver says it's "like what you might call stage fright [...]. I was awkward as hell."[[216]](#cite_note-216) The physiological effects of limerence include [trembling](/wiki/Trembling "Trembling"), [pallor](/wiki/Pallor "Pallor"), [flushing](/wiki/Flushing_(physiology) "Flushing (physiology)"), [weakness](/wiki/Weakness "Weakness"), [sweating](/wiki/Sweating "Sweating"), [butterflies in the stomach](/wiki/Butterflies_in_the_stomach "Butterflies in the stomach") and a [pounding heart](/wiki/Palpitations "Palpitations").[[215]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_49-215)[[217]](#cite_note-Fisher_2016_22-217) When Tennov asked her informants where the sensation of limerence was felt, "they pointed unerringly to the midpoint in their chest. So consistently did this occur that it would seem to be another indication that the state described is indeed limerence".[[218]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_64-218)

In a 1987 survey by [Shere Hite](/wiki/Shere_Hite "Shere Hite") in which many participants described relationships which were clearly limerent, 69% of married women and 48% of single women "neither liked, nor trusted, being in love", and their responses indicated being in love was mostly distressing. 17% "could no longer take love seriously".[[219]](#cite_note-219) Tennov describes being under the spell herself: "Before it happened, I couldn't have imagined it[.] Now, I wouldn't want to have it happen again."[[13]](#cite_note-wapo1990-13) Some people even described to her incidents of [self-harm](/wiki/Self-harm "Self-harm"), but Tennov maintains that such tragedies involve limerence "augmented and distorted" by other factors.[[220]](#cite_note-220)

There's debate among academics over when love can be considered [addiction](/wiki/Addiction "Addiction"), and whether addiction is really a "true" [mental illness](/wiki/Mental_disorder "Mental disorder").[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8)[[52]](#cite_note-tallis-addict-52) Lovesickness has been pathologized in previous centuries, but is not currently in the [ICD-10](/wiki/ICD-10 "ICD-10"), [ICPC](/wiki/International_Classification_of_Primary_Care "International Classification of Primary Care") or [DSM-5](/wiki/DSM-5 "DSM-5").[[35]](#cite_note-ethnopharma-35)[[221]](#cite_note-autogenerated12-221) The lovers described by Tennov bear a particular resemblance to addicts, but limerence was not intended to denote an abnormal state.[[52]](#cite_note-tallis-addict-52) The author and [clinical psychologist](/wiki/Clinical_psychologist "Clinical psychologist") [Frank Tallis](/wiki/Frank_Tallis "Frank Tallis") has made the argument that all love—even normal love—is largely indistinguishable from mental illness.[[222]](#cite_note-222)

> For should I see thee a little moment,  
>     Straight is my voice hushed;  
> Yea, my tongue is broken, and  
>     through and through me  
> 'Neath the flesh, impalpable fire  
>     runs tingling;  
> Nothing see mine eyes, and a  
>     voice of roaring  
> Waves in my ear sounds;  
>     Sweat runs down in rivers, a  
>     tremor seizes  
> All my limbs, and paler than  
>     grass in autumn,  
> Caught by pains of menacing  
>     death, I falter,  
> Lost in the love-trance.

[Sappho](/wiki/Sappho "Sappho")[[223]](#cite_note-223)

The symptoms of lovesickness still bear resemblance to entries in the DSM, which now includes some addictions, and there are other entries which also resemble core symptoms of [falling in love](/wiki/Falling_in_love "Falling in love"): preoccupation, episodes of melancholy, rapture and instability of mood.[[224]](#cite_note-224)[[221]](#cite_note-autogenerated12-221) These correspond to conventional diagnoses of obsessionality (or [OCD](/wiki/Obsessive%E2%80%93compulsive_disorder "Obsessive–compulsive disorder")), [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)"), [mania](/wiki/Mania "Mania") (or [hypomania](/wiki/Hypomania "Hypomania")) and [manic depression](/wiki/Bipolar_disorder "Bipolar disorder").[[225]](#cite_note-225)[[221]](#cite_note-autogenerated12-221) Other examples are physical symptoms resembling [panic attacks](/wiki/Panic_attack "Panic attack") ([pounding heart](/wiki/Palpitations "Palpitations"), [trembling](/wiki/Tremor "Tremor"), [shortness of breath](/wiki/Shortness_of_breath "Shortness of breath") and [lightheadedness](/wiki/Lightheadedness "Lightheadedness")), excessive worrying about the future resembling [generalized anxiety disorder](/wiki/Generalized_anxiety_disorder "Generalized anxiety disorder"), appetite disturbance and sensitivity about one's appearance resembling [anorexia nervosa](/wiki/Anorexia_nervosa "Anorexia nervosa"), and the feeling that life has become a dream resembling [derealization](/wiki/Derealization "Derealization") and [depersonalization](/wiki/Depersonalization "Depersonalization").[[226]](#cite_note-226)

It has been argued that falling in love is involuntary, but whether one's subsequent [behavior](/wiki/Behavior "Behavior") could be considered [autonomous](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy") may depend on whether addictive love is viewed as a normal or abnormal state.[[8]](#cite_note-:3-8) Tallis argues that love evolved to override [rationality](/wiki/Rationality "Rationality") so that one finds a lover to reproduce with, regardless of the personal costs of bearing and raising a child:[[227]](#cite_note-227)

> At first sight, it seems extraordinary that evolutionary forces might conspire to shape something that looks like a mental illness to ensure reproductive success. Yet, there are many reasons why love should have evolved to share with madness several features—the most notable of which is the loss of reason. Like the ancient [humoral](/wiki/Humorism "Humorism") model of love sickness, evolutionary principles seem to have necessitated a blurring of the distinction between normal and abnormal states. Evolution expects us to love madly, lest we fail to love at all.

According to Tennov, "Love has been called a madness and an affliction at least since the time of the [ancient Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greeks "Ancient Greeks") and probably earlier than that."[[228]](#cite_note-228) Historically, lovesickness has been attributed to arrows shot by [Eros](/wiki/Eros "Eros"), a sickness entering through the eyes (like [evil eye](/wiki/Evil_eye "Evil eye")), excess of [black bile](/wiki/Black_bile "Black bile"), [spells](/wiki/Spell_(magic) "Spell (magic)"), [potions](/wiki/Love_potion "Love potion") and other [magic](/wiki/Magic_(supernatural) "Magic (supernatural)"). The first known treatise on the subject is *[Remedia Amoris](/wiki/Remedia_Amoris "Remedia Amoris")*, by the poet [Ovid](/wiki/Ovid "Ovid"). People have tried to treat lovesickness with a variety of natural products, charms and rituals.[[35]](#cite_note-ethnopharma-35) The [bioethicist](/wiki/Bioethics "Bioethics") [Brian Earp](/wiki/Brian_Earp "Brian Earp") and his colleagues have argued that the voluntary use of anti-love drugs (made to cause a person to fall out of love) could be ethical, but there's no drug now which is a realistic candidate.[[229]](#cite_note-229)[[230]](#cite_note-230)[[231]](#cite_note-refuting-231)[[35]](#cite_note-ethnopharma-35)

### Intrusive thinking and fantasy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Intrusive thinking and fantasy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/OCD_Cycle.svg/250px-OCD_Cycle.svg.png)](/wiki/File:OCD_Cycle.svg)

The obsessional features of [passionate love](/wiki/Passionate_love "Passionate love") (as in limerence) have been compared to [OCD](/wiki/OCD "OCD") since the late 1990s, although with differences.[[44]](#cite_note-fisher1998-44)[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[138]](#cite_note-co-opted-138)[[232]](#cite_note-bellamy-ocd-232) Unlike OCD, limerence starts with a period of intoxicating joy, and only later reaches a state of [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety") when [unrequited](/wiki/Unrequited_love "Unrequited love").[[232]](#cite_note-bellamy-ocd-232) The thoughts also differ in function and content.[[138]](#cite_note-co-opted-138)In [addiction](/wiki/Addiction "Addiction"), the early stage starts with [positive reinforcement](/wiki/Positive_reinforcement "Positive reinforcement") (binging and [intoxication](/wiki/Substance_intoxication "Substance intoxication")), but over time a transition occurs towards a more [compulsive](/wiki/Compulsive "Compulsive") stage of [negative reinforcement](/wiki/Negative_reinforcement "Negative reinforcement") (avoiding [withdrawal](/wiki/Drug_withdrawal "Drug withdrawal")).[[233]](#cite_note-koob-volkow-233)[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[234]](#cite_note-234)[[52]](#cite_note-tallis-addict-52) This later stage (of negative reinforcement) is a possible parallel with OCD (where compulsions relieve tension or anxiety).[[235]](#cite_note-235)[[233]](#cite_note-koob-volkow-233)[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)

[Intrusive thinking](/wiki/Intrusive_thought "Intrusive thought") is a hallmark or cardinal trait of romantic love.[[138]](#cite_note-co-opted-138)[[128]](#cite_note-:25-128)[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[236]](#cite_note-langeslag2012-236) Tennov wrote that "Limerence is first and foremost a condition of cognitive obsession."[[237]](#cite_note-237) One study found that on average people in love spent 65% of their waking hours thinking of their beloved.[[236]](#cite_note-langeslag2012-236) [Arthur Aron](/wiki/Arthur_Aron "Arthur Aron") says "It is obsessive-compulsive when you're feeling it. It's the center of your life."[[27]](#cite_note-usatoday-27) At the height of obsessive fantasy, a person in limerence can spend 85 to nearly 100% of their days and nights doting in reverie, lose their ability to focus and become distracted.[[54]](#cite_note-Fisher_2016_21-54)

A limerent person can spend time fantasizing about future events even if they never come true, as the anticipation on its own yields [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine").[[38]](#cite_note-mccracken-38) According to Tennov, limerent fantasy is unsatisfactory unless rooted in reality, because the fantasy must seem realistic enough to be somewhat possible.[[238]](#cite_note-238)[[239]](#cite_note-239) Fantasies can nevertheless be wildly unrealistic: one person recalled an elaborate rescue, in which he saves an LO's 5-year-old cousin from motorcycles, only to be killed by a snake in the lap of his LO as she tells him "I love you".[[240]](#cite_note-240) This fantasizing along with the replaying of actual memories forms a bridge between ordinary life and the eventual hoped-for moment: consummation. Tennov says that limerent fantasy is "inescapable", something that just "happens" as opposed to something one "does".[[241]](#cite_note-241)

[Ellen Berscheid](/wiki/Ellen_S._Berscheid "Ellen S. Berscheid") & [Elaine Hatfield](/wiki/Elaine_Hatfield "Elaine Hatfield") (cited by Tennov)[[242]](#cite_note-242) state on the importance of fantasy:[[243]](#cite_note-243)

> When the lover closes his eyes and daydreams, he can summon up a flawless partner—a partner who instantaneously satisfies all his unspoken, conflicting, and fleeting desires. In fantasy he may receive unlimited reward or he may *anticipate* that he would receive unlimited reward were he ever to actually meet his ideal. Compared to our grandiose fantasies, the level of reward we receive in our real interactions is severely circumscribed. As a consequence, sometimes the most extreme passion is aroused by partners who exist only in imagination or partners who are barely known.

One theory of obsessive thinking draws a parallel with [drug addiction](/wiki/Drug_addiction "Drug addiction"): the early stage of romantic love is compared to addiction, and drug addicts also exhibit obsessive thoughts about drug use.[[204]](#cite_note-:7-204)[[52]](#cite_note-tallis-addict-52) Tennov conceived of limerent fantasy (based in reality) as "intricate strategy planning".[[244]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_247-244) In the late 1990s, it was also speculated that falling in love lowered [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin") levels in the brain, believed to cause intrusive thoughts.[[44]](#cite_note-fisher1998-44)[[245]](#cite_note-marazziti-245) This was based on a comparison to [obsessive–compulsive disorder](/wiki/Obsessive%E2%80%93compulsive_disorder "Obsessive–compulsive disorder"), but the experiments were ambiguous.[[246]](#cite_note-leckmanmayes-246)[[245]](#cite_note-marazziti-245)[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6) The experiments also measured [blood](/wiki/Blood "Blood") levels rather than in the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system"), making the results difficult to interpret. The first experiment found that [serotonin transporter](/wiki/Serotonin_transporter "Serotonin transporter") levels were lower, but a second experiment found that blood serotonin levels in men and women were affected differently.[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[236]](#cite_note-langeslag2012-236)[[245]](#cite_note-marazziti-245) This second experiment found that obsessive thinking was actually associated with increased serotonin in women.[[236]](#cite_note-langeslag2012-236) [SSRI](/wiki/Selective_serotonin_reuptake_inhibitor "Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor") use also seemed to not have an effect on obsessive thinking in a 2025 study.[[247]](#cite_note-:28-247)

For some people who [fear intimacy](/wiki/Fear_of_intimacy "Fear of intimacy") or have a history of [trauma](/wiki/Psychological_trauma "Psychological trauma"), limerent fantasy might be an escape, without the threat of real intimacy.[[184]](#cite_note-:18-184)[[248]](#cite_note-:17-248)

### Crystallization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Crystallization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Crystaled_salt_on_the_stick.jpg/250px-Crystaled_salt_on_the_stick.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crystaled_salt_on_the_stick.jpg)
