Crystallized salt

[Crystallization](/wiki/Crystallization_(love) "Crystallization (love)"), for Tennov, is the "remarkable ability to emphasize what is truly admirable in LO and to avoid dwelling on the negative, even to respond with a compassion for the negative and render it, emotionally if not perceptually, into another positive attribute."[[249]](#cite_note-249)[[44]](#cite_note-fisher1998-44) The term comes from the French writer [Stendhal's](/wiki/Stendhal "Stendhal") 1821 treatise on love, [*De l'Amour*](/wiki/De_l%27amour_(Stendhal) "De l'amour (Stendhal)"), in which he describes an analogy where a tree branch is tossed into a [salt mine](/wiki/Salt_mining "Salt mining"). After several months, the tree branch (or twig) becomes covered in salt crystals which transform it "into an object of shimmering beauty". In the same way, unattractive characteristics of an LO are given little to no attention, so that the LO is seen in the most favorable light.[[250]](#cite_note-250)[[251]](#cite_note-251) One of Tennov's informants says:[[252]](#cite_note-252)

> Yes I knew he gambled, I knew he sometimes drank too much, and I knew he didn't read a book from one year to the next. *I knew* and I didn't know. [...] I dwelt on his wavy hair, the way he looked at me, the thought of his driving to work in the morning, his charm (that I believed must surely affect everyone he met), the flowers he sent, [...]. Okay! I know it's crazy, that my list of 'positives' sounds silly, but those *are* the things I think of, remember, and, yes, want back again!

This kind of "misperception" or "love is blind" [cognitive bias](/wiki/Cognitive_bias "Cognitive bias")[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[54]](#cite_note-Fisher_2016_21-54) is more often referred to as "idealization",[[253]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_31-253) which modern research considers to be a form of [positive illusions](/wiki/Positive_illusions "Positive illusions").[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[254]](#cite_note-murray1996-254) Past authors have sometimes depicted idealization as a malady, but significant scientific evidence has shown that positive illusions actually contribute to relationship satisfaction, long-term well-being and decreased risk for relationship discontinuation.[[254]](#cite_note-murray1996-254)[[255]](#cite_note-song-positive-255)[[256]](#cite_note-fisher-positive-256) Tennov argues against the term "idealization", because she says it implies that the image seen by the person experiencing romantic passion "is molded to fit a preformed, externally derived, or emotionally needed conception".[[253]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_31-253) In crystallization, the term she prefers, "the actual and existing features of LO merely undergo enhancement."[[253]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_31-253)

A limerent person may overlook red flags or incompatibilities.[[248]](#cite_note-:17-248)[[85]](#cite_note-:30-85) Crystallization can be an impediment to recovery, as one of Tennov's informants relates:[[257]](#cite_note-257)

> I decided to make a list in block letters of everything about Elsie that I found unpleasant or annoying. It was a very long list. On the other side of the paper, I listed her good points. It was a short list. But it didn't help at all. The good points seemed *so much more important*, and the bad things, well, in Elsie they weren't so bad, or they were things I felt I could help her with.

### Readiness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Readiness")]

Some people have a heightened susceptibility to limerence, a state Tennov calls "readiness", "longing for limerence" or being "in love with love".[[258]](#cite_note-258)[[259]](#cite_note-:21-259) This can occur due to biological factors (like [adolescence](/wiki/Adolescence "Adolescence")), but also psychological factors (like [loneliness](/wiki/Loneliness "Loneliness") or discontent). Sometimes readiness can be so intense that a person [falls in love](/wiki/Falling_in_love "Falling in love") with somebody with only minimal appeal.[[259]](#cite_note-:21-259) The psychoanalysts [Freud](/wiki/Sigmund_Freud "Sigmund Freud") and [Reik](/wiki/Theodor_Reik "Theodor Reik") believed that [unhappy](/wiki/Unhappiness "Unhappiness") people tend to be the most vulnerable to love and fantasy; [Elaine Hatfield](/wiki/Elaine_Hatfield "Elaine Hatfield") concurs, saying "the greater our need, the more grandiose our fantasies".[[260]](#cite_note-260)[[259]](#cite_note-:21-259)[[261]](#cite_note-261) These [clinical theorists](/wiki/Clinical_psychology "Clinical psychology") have been interpreted as dealing with the same ("hot, passionate") aspects of love as Tennov, although they were writing before her.[[262]](#cite_note-sternberg-liking-loving-262)

[Shaver](/wiki/Phillip_R._Shaver "Phillip R. Shaver") & [Hazan](/wiki/Cindy_Hazan "Cindy Hazan") observed that lonely people are more susceptible to limerence,[[263]](#cite_note-263) arguing that if people have many unmet social needs and are unaware, then a sign somebody is interested in them may become magnified into something quite unrealistic.[[264]](#cite_note-264)

### Uncertainty and hope

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Uncertainty and hope")]

See also: [Reward theory of attraction § Reinforcement schedules](/wiki/Reward_theory_of_attraction#Reinforcement_schedules "Reward theory of attraction")

According to [Dorothy Tennov](/wiki/Dorothy_Tennov "Dorothy Tennov"), as an elaboration on a theory by [Stendhal](/wiki/Stendhal "Stendhal"), "uncertainty" is a key element to limerence:[[265]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_56-265)[[266]](#cite_note-266)

> The recognition that some uncertainty must exist has been commented on and complained about by virtually everyone who has undertaken a serious study of the phenomenon of romantic love. Psychologists [Ellen Berscheid](/wiki/Ellen_S._Berscheid "Ellen S. Berscheid") and [Elaine Walster](/wiki/Elaine_Hatfield "Elaine Hatfield") discussed this common observation made, they note, by [Socrates](/wiki/Socrates "Socrates"), [Ovid](/wiki/Ovid "Ovid"), the *[Kama Sutra](/wiki/Kama_Sutra "Kama Sutra")*, and "[Dear Abby](/wiki/Dear_Abby "Dear Abby")," that the presentation of a hard-to-get as opposed to an immediately yielding exterior is a help in eliciting passion.

Rather than being an [emotion](/wiki/Emotion "Emotion") itself, [romantic love](/wiki/Romantic_love "Romantic love") is a [motivational](/wiki/Motivation "Motivation") state which elicits different emotions depending on the situation:[[231]](#cite_note-refuting-231)[[44]](#cite_note-fisher1998-44) positive feelings when things go well and negative feelings when things go awry.[[57]](#cite_note-:4-57) The "goal" according to Tennov's analysis is "oneness" with the LO, i.e. mutual reciprocation or return of feelings; a person in limerence is emotionally dependent on the perceived probability of this reciprocation.[[267]](#cite_note-267) In the [reward theory of attraction](/wiki/Reward_theory_of_attraction "Reward theory of attraction"), an LO is seen as a [reinforcer](/wiki/Reinforcement "Reinforcement") because of the potential for a rewarding experience in their presence.[[262]](#cite_note-sternberg-liking-loving-262)[[268]](#cite_note-268)[[269]](#cite_note-269)

According to Tennov's theory, two elements are required for limerence to develop and intensify: hope and uncertainty. There must be at least some hope that an LO will reciprocate, but uncertainty over their true feelings is required for the preoccupation and mood changes to intensify.[[270]](#cite_note-270) In some cases, uncertain reciprocation can produce mood swings which are so abrupt as to cause emotional volatility, even in generally stable people. One of Tennov's informants recalls: "When I felt [Barry] loved me, I was intensely in love and deliriously happy; when he seemed rejecting, I was still intensely in love, only miserable beyond words."[[271]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_44-271)

Limerence normally subsides when either:[[59]](#cite_note-conceptions-of-limerence-59)[[272]](#cite_note-272)

1. all hope of reciprocation is ended;
2. the limerent person enters a relationship with the LO and receives adequate reciprocation;
3. limerence is "transferred" to a different LO.

In even some further cases ("and this is the madness of it", Tennov says), the [lovesickness](/wiki/Lovesickness "Lovesickness") and [intrusive thoughts](/wiki/Intrusive_thought "Intrusive thought") can *still* remain, even after all hope is exhausted and the sufferer wants to be rid of the state.[[61]](#cite_note-:31-61) After a transition to [addiction](/wiki/Addiction "Addiction"), the [executive brain](/wiki/Executive_functions "Executive functions") is sidestepped, and some reactions and behavioral [habits](/wiki/Habit "Habit") become essentially automatic.[[273]](#cite_note-273)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Souhtland-Casino.jpg/250px-Souhtland-Casino.jpg)](/wiki/File:Souhtland-Casino.jpg)

"Both gamblers and limerents find reason to hope in wild dreams." —[Dorothy Tennov](/wiki/Dorothy_Tennov "Dorothy Tennov")[[274]](#cite_note-274)

The uncertainty of limerence has been interpreted as [intermittent reinforcement](/wiki/Intermittent_reinforcement "Intermittent reinforcement") by [Robert Sternberg](/wiki/Robert_Sternberg "Robert Sternberg"),[[262]](#cite_note-sternberg-liking-loving-262) keeping the brain "hooked" in.[[38]](#cite_note-mccracken-38) When people behave inconsistently or contrary to expectations, this can spark interest and be a fuel for [passion](/wiki/Passion_(emotion) "Passion (emotion)") (either [ecstasy](/wiki/Ecstasy_(emotion) "Ecstasy (emotion)") or [agony](/wiki/Suffering "Suffering")).[[275]](#cite_note-275) This relies on a mechanic of [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine"), which does not encode reward per se, but rather encodes a "reward prediction error" signal: whether a given reward is better than, equal to, or worse than expected.[[276]](#cite_note-bellamy-slots-276)[[277]](#cite_note-277)[[278]](#cite_note-schultz2016-278)[[279]](#cite_note-schultz2000-279) A [slot machine](/wiki/Slot_machine "Slot machine") involves a comparable situation, where the rewards are designed to be always unpredictable so the [gambler](/wiki/Gambler "Gambler") cannot understand the pattern. Unable to [habituate](/wiki/Habituation "Habituation") to the experience, for some people the exhilarating high from the unexpected wins leads to [gambling addiction](/wiki/Gambling_addiction "Gambling addiction") and [compulsions](/wiki/Compulsive_behavior "Compulsive behavior"). If the machine paid out on a regular interval (so that the rewards were expected), it would not be as exciting.[[276]](#cite_note-bellamy-slots-276) The uncertainty of receiving an occasional [message](/wiki/Message "Message") from an LO is "gasoline poured on the fire", according to [Judson Brewer](/wiki/Judson_A._Brewer "Judson A. Brewer").[[38]](#cite_note-mccracken-38)

Uncertainty can also be introduced by the presence of barriers to a relationship, like parental interference or a deceived spouse.[[280]](#cite_note-280) This "intensification through adversity" was crucial to the mutual limerence of [Romeo and Juliet](/wiki/Romeo_and_Juliet "Romeo and Juliet"), hence this is often called "the *Romeo and Juliet* effect".[[84]](#cite_note-Tennov_57-84) [Helen Fisher](/wiki/Helen_Fisher_(anthropologist) "Helen Fisher (anthropologist)") called it "frustration attraction",[[54]](#cite_note-Fisher_2016_21-54)[[281]](#cite_note-281) and believed that separation evokes [panic](/wiki/Panic "Panic") and [stress](/wiki/Stress_(biology) "Stress (biology)"), which activates the [hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal axis](/wiki/Hypothalamic%E2%80%93pituitary%E2%80%93adrenal_axis "Hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal axis"). It's ironic, she says, because this can also produce dopamine, so "as the adored one slips away, the very chemicals that contribute to feelings of romance grow even more potent".[[282]](#cite_note-Fisher_2016_21–22-282)[[283]](#cite_note-283) According to Tennov, "It is limerence, not love, that increases when lovers are able to meet only infrequently or when there is anger between them."[[91]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_71-91)

One can attempt to extinguish limerence by removing any hope that an LO will reciprocate.[[284]](#cite_note-284)[[59]](#cite_note-conceptions-of-limerence-59)[[262]](#cite_note-sternberg-liking-loving-262) An individual who is the object of unwanted limerent attraction should give the clearest possible rejection, rather than something ambiguous such as "I like you as a friend, but...".[[285]](#cite_note-285)

### Ecstatic union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Ecstatic union")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/ISS-40_Thunderheads_near_Borneo.jpg/250px-ISS-40_Thunderheads_near_Borneo.jpg)](/wiki/File:ISS-40_Thunderheads_near_Borneo.jpg)

"I felt as though the clouds were not on the horizon but under my feet. How sweet it was." —[Liv Ullmann](/wiki/Liv_Ullmann "Liv Ullmann")[[286]](#cite_note-286)

Although limerence is usually [unrequited](/wiki/Unrequited_love "Unrequited love"), it can lead to a [relationship](/wiki/Intimate_relationship "Intimate relationship") in some cases.[[287]](#cite_note-287)[[212]](#cite_note-thelovedrug-212)[[3]](#cite_note-Hayes-3)[[59]](#cite_note-conceptions-of-limerence-59) According to Tennov's theory and observations, small doses of attention from an LO (along with uncertainty) increase the intensity of limerence, and a sensation of buoyancy or "walking on air" is felt when reciprocation seems near.[[58]](#cite_note-:33-58)[[288]](#cite_note-288) "Reciprocation leads to euphoria, followed by a union that might be stable or unstable, and that might or might not endure."[[58]](#cite_note-:33-58) This "ecstatic union" (a phrase coined by [Simone de Beauvoir](/wiki/Simone_de_Beauvoir "Simone de Beauvoir")) is recalled by one of Tennov's informants: "The landlord had given me notice and the bank loan had not gone through, and I could not bring myself to care! Whatever happened, it would be wonderful somehow. My delight in simply existing eclipsed everything else".[[289]](#cite_note-289) 95% of her survey group called love "a beautiful experience".[[290]](#cite_note-290)

A 2025 study of the largest cross-cultural survey of *currently* in-love people (who were also in relationships) categorized 29.42% of their sample as "intense" romantic lovers, and 28.57% of those fell in love before their relationship. (That is, only 8.4% of the study were *both* intensely in love *and* also fell in love before their relationship. The majority of intense lovers fell in love *after* their relationship started, with one month after being the average.)[[291]](#cite_note-291)

Normally then, limerence diminishes inside a relationship, with reciprocity.[[292]](#cite_note-292)[[59]](#cite_note-conceptions-of-limerence-59) Desire fades because of a [habituation](/wiki/Habituation "Habituation") effect on [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") activity: as a reward is more easily and predictably obtained, the dopamine release in response to reward cues decreases.[[293]](#cite_note-293)[[294]](#cite_note-294) Research also suggests that [oxytocin](/wiki/Oxytocin "Oxytocin") activity might inhibit the more excessive effects of [addiction](/wiki/Addiction "Addiction"), with oxytocin from the [attachment](/wiki/Attachment_theory "Attachment theory") system being more active in reciprocated, well-functioning relationships compared to unrequited situations.[[204]](#cite_note-:7-204)[[295]](#cite_note-295)[[138]](#cite_note-co-opted-138) In some cases, however, just getting into a relationship by itself may still not be enough for limerence to diminish, if reciprocation is insufficient.[[59]](#cite_note-conceptions-of-limerence-59)[[296]](#cite_note-:34-296) According to Tennov, reciprocation must be "sustained and believable", else limerence can continue inside a relationship if the partner (LO) behaves in a nonlimerent way.[[59]](#cite_note-conceptions-of-limerence-59) Limerence and uncertainty theory have also been interpreted in terms of [attachment](/wiki/Attachment_theory "Attachment theory") anxiety, worsening the symptoms.[[297]](#cite_note-what-fuels-passion-297)[[147]](#cite_note-:36-147) This can be caused *both* by an [anxious attachment](/wiki/Anxious-preoccupied_attachment "Anxious-preoccupied attachment") style *or* a situation where (for example) an [avoidant](/wiki/Avoidant_attachment "Avoidant attachment") partner can make a normally [secure](/wiki/Secure_attachment "Secure attachment") person feel and act anxious (as in the [person–situation debate](/wiki/Person%E2%80%93situation_debate "Person–situation debate")).[[47]](#cite_note-hazanshaver-47)[[298]](#cite_note-298) One man interviewed by Tennov described being caught in one-sided limerence with his [wife](/wiki/Wife "Wife") "in constant fear of [divorce](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce")" for 25 years (until she died); later, however, he found a different partner whom he did not have this reaction to.[[296]](#cite_note-:34-296)[[299]](#cite_note-299)[[259]](#cite_note-:21-259) Under the attachment view, [passion](/wiki/Passion_(emotion) "Passion (emotion)") wanes as a relationship becomes more secure (as uncertainty is reduced).[[262]](#cite_note-sternberg-liking-loving-262)

The *[Passionate Love Scale](/wiki/Passionate_Love_Scale "Passionate Love Scale")* obsession factor (compared to limerence) has been [correlated](/wiki/Correlation "Correlation") with relationship satisfaction in short-term relationships; however, studies have also found that as romantic obsession continues inside a relationship over a longer time, the correlation is with decreased satisfaction. This is speculated to be due to low [self-esteem](/wiki/Self-esteem "Self-esteem") and insecure or anxious attachment.[[33]](#cite_note-acevedo2009-33)[[300]](#cite_note-:110-300) Other studies have found that anxious attachment [mediated](/wiki/Mediation_(statistics) "Mediation (statistics)") the relationship between [neuroticism](/wiki/Neuroticism "Neuroticism") and its related [love styles](/wiki/Colour_wheel_theory_of_love "Colour wheel theory of love"), and the [mania](/wiki/Colour_wheel_theory_of_love#Mania "Colour wheel theory of love") love attitude (which has been related to limerence) mediated the relationship between neuroticism and relationship satisfaction.[[301]](#cite_note-301)[[48]](#cite_note-feeneynoller-48)

In some cases, limerence can be extinguished quite quickly after a relationship is established, because with more routine contact the participants begin to notice things they don't like about each other.[[3]](#cite_note-Hayes-3)[[262]](#cite_note-sternberg-liking-loving-262) A reminiscent concept from [triangular theory of love](/wiki/Triangular_theory_of_love "Triangular theory of love") is "fatuous love" (passion, with commitment, without intimacy): as in new passionate lovers who commit to [marry](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") without really knowing each other. Usually this fatuous passion fades and turns into just an unhappy commitment by itself, called "empty love".[[302]](#cite_note-302) The [philosopher](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") [Bertrand Russell](/wiki/Bertrand_Russell "Bertrand Russell") is quoted by Tennov in her discussion of uncertainty,[[265]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_56-265) quipping that "when a man has no difficulty in obtaining a woman, his feeling towards her does not take the form of romantic love", but Russell goes on to say that "I think it is good—that romantic love should form the motive for a marriage, but it should be understood that the kind of love which will enable a marriage to remain happy and to fulfil its social purpose is not romantic but is something more intimate, affectionate, and realistic. In romantic love the beloved object is not seen accurately, but through a glamorous mist".[[303]](#cite_note-303)

According to Tennov, ideally limerence will be replaced by another type of love.[[59]](#cite_note-conceptions-of-limerence-59) In this way, feelings may evolve: "Those whose limerence was replaced by affectional bonding with the same partner might say, 'We were very much in love when we married; today we love each other very much.'"[[304]](#cite_note-304) The more stable type of love which is usually the characteristic of long-term relationships is commonly called [companionate love](/wiki/Companionate_love "Companionate love"), [storge](/wiki/Storge "Storge") or [attachment](/wiki/Attachment_theory "Attachment theory").[[33]](#cite_note-acevedo2009-33)[[24]](#cite_note-4th-dim-24)[[46]](#cite_note-fisher2002-46) An [fMRI](/wiki/FMRI "FMRI") experiment of people who were in happy, long-term relationships (10 years or more) but professed to still be "madly" in love found brain activations in dopamine-rich reward areas (interpreted as "wanting" or "desire for union"), but also activity in the [globus palludus](/wiki/Globus_pallidus "Globus pallidus"), a site for [opiate](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid") receptors identified as a hedonic hotspot ("liking"). Unlike people who were newly in love, these participants also did not show activity in areas associated with [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety") and [fear](/wiki/Fear "Fear"), and reported far less obsessional features.[[133]](#cite_note-time-marriage-133)[[131]](#cite_note-:42-131)[[33]](#cite_note-acevedo2009-33)

### Duration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Duration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Crumbs.jpg/250px-Crumbs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crumbs.jpg)

"Limerence can live a long life sustained by crumbs." —[Dorothy Tennov](/wiki/Dorothy_Tennov "Dorothy Tennov")[[305]](#cite_note-305)

Tennov estimates based on her questionnaire and interviews that limerence most frequently lasts between 18 months and 3 years, with an average of 2 years, but may be as short as mere days or as long as a lifetime.[[306]](#cite_note-306) One woman wrote to Tennov about her mother's limerence which lasted 65 years.[[13]](#cite_note-wapo1990-13) Tennov calls it the worst case when the limerent person cannot get away, because the LO is a coworker or lives nearby.[[13]](#cite_note-wapo1990-13) Limerence can last indefinitely sometimes when [unrequited](/wiki/Unrequited_love "Unrequited love"), especially when reciprocation is uncertain: with [intermittent reinforcement](/wiki/Intermittent_reinforcement "Intermittent reinforcement") and mixed signals, for example, an LO ignoring the limerent person for awhile and then suddenly calling.[[212]](#cite_note-thelovedrug-212)[[262]](#cite_note-sternberg-liking-loving-262)[[38]](#cite_note-mccracken-38) Stringing a limerent person along with intermittent communication is called "[breadcrumbing](/wiki/Breadcrumbing "Breadcrumbing")".[[38]](#cite_note-mccracken-38)[[307]](#cite_note-307)

Tennov's estimate of 18 months to 3 years is sometimes used as the normal duration of [romantic love](/wiki/Romantic_love "Romantic love").[[308]](#cite_note-:1-308)[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6) The other common estimate, 12–18 months, comes from Donatella Marazziti's experiment comparing [serotonin transporter](/wiki/Serotonin_transporter "Serotonin transporter") levels of people in love with [OCD](/wiki/Obsessive%E2%80%93compulsive_disorder "Obsessive–compulsive disorder") patients.[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7)[[245]](#cite_note-marazziti-245) In this experiment, subjects who had fallen in love within the past 6 months (who were in a relationship) were measured to have [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin") transporter levels which were different from controls, levels which returned to normal after 12–18 months.[[245]](#cite_note-marazziti-245)

## Love regulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Love regulation")]

Love regulation, studied by the [psychologist](/wiki/Psychologist "Psychologist") [Sandra Langeslag](/wiki/Sandra_Langeslag "Sandra Langeslag"), is "the use of behavioral or cognitive strategies to change the intensity of current feelings of romantic love".[[309]](#cite_note-regulating-309) Langeslag works with [Helen Fisher's](/wiki/Helen_Fisher_(anthropologist) "Helen Fisher (anthropologist)") model (*lust*, *attraction* and *attachment*, i.e. [independent emotion systems](/wiki/Biology_of_romantic_love#Independent_emotion_systems "Biology of romantic love")), but uses the terms infatuation (i.e. passionate love) and attachment (i.e. companionate love).[[310]](#cite_note-310)[[231]](#cite_note-refuting-231) It's a common misconception that love feelings are *uncontrollable*, or even should not be controlled; however studies using [EEG](/wiki/Electroencephalography "Electroencephalography") and [psychometrics](/wiki/Psychometrics "Psychometrics") have shown that love regulation is possible and can be useful.[[231]](#cite_note-refuting-231)[[309]](#cite_note-regulating-309)

In a technique called cognitive reappraisal, one focuses on positive or negative aspects of their beloved, the relationship, or imagined future scenarios:

* In *positive* reappraisal, one focuses on positive qualities of the beloved ("he's kind", "she's spontaneous"), the relationship ("we have so much fun together") or imagined future scenarios ("we'll live happily ever after").[[309]](#cite_note-regulating-309)[[311]](#cite_note-valentine-311) Positive reappraisal increases attachment and can increase relationship satisfaction.[[311]](#cite_note-valentine-311)
* In *negative* reappraisal, one focuses on negative qualities of the beloved ("he's lazy", "she's always late"), the relationship ("we fight a lot") or imagined future scenarios ("he'll cheat on me").[[309]](#cite_note-regulating-309)[[311]](#cite_note-valentine-311) Negative reappraisal decreases feelings of infatuation and attachment, but decreases mood in the short term. Langeslag has recommended distraction as an antidote to the short-term decrease in mood.[[311]](#cite_note-valentine-311)[[312]](#cite_note-times-312)

Preliminary results from a 2024 study of online limerence communities conducted by Langeslag found that negative reappraisal decreased limerence for the study participants.[[313]](#cite_note-:5-313)[[314]](#cite_note-314) A therapist named Brandy Wyant has also had her limerent clients list reasons their LO is not perfect, or reasons they and their LO are not compatible.[[38]](#cite_note-mccracken-38) Love regulation doesn't switch feelings on or off immediately, so Langeslag recommends writing a list of things once a day as an example.[[315]](#cite_note-bestway2-315)

The [neuroscientist](/wiki/Neuroscientist "Neuroscientist") Tom Bellamy is recommending what he calls the "daymare" strategy: if a person in limerence finds themselves lost in a romantic daydream, they should "spoil the rewards" by changing the end of the story into a nightmare.[[316]](#cite_note-316)[[317]](#cite_note-:39-317) Ruining reward-seeking habits like this is recommended as a kind of "deprogramming" to "accelerate the overwriting of memories linking LO to reward".[[317]](#cite_note-:39-317)

Based on the addiction theory of romantic love, [Helen Fisher](/wiki/Helen_Fisher_(anthropologist) "Helen Fisher (anthropologist)") and colleagues recommend that rejected lovers remove all reminders of their beloved, such as letters or photos, and avoid contact with the rejecting partner. Reminders can cause cravings which prolong recovery. They also suggest that positive contact with friends could reduce cravings. Rejected lovers should stay busy to distract themselves, and engage in self-expanding activities.[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7) Setting a "no contact" rule during recovery can facilitate self-care and time to reflect on the situation.[[318]](#cite_note-318)

However, if limerence is being sustained by a fantasy (making the reward more potent), then getting to know the person for real can also be the fastest way to get over them.[[319]](#cite_note-319)

## Controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerence&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Controversy")]

See also: [Obsessive love](/wiki/Obsessive_love "Obsessive love") and [Biology of romantic love § Obsessive thinking](/wiki/Biology_of_romantic_love#Obsessive_thinking "Biology of romantic love")

In 2008, Albert Wakin, a professor who knew Tennov at the [University of Bridgeport](/wiki/University_of_Bridgeport "University of Bridgeport") but did not assist in her research, and Duyen Vo, a graduate student, suggested that limerence is similar to [obsessive–compulsive disorder (OCD)](/wiki/Obsessive%E2%80%93compulsive_disorder "Obsessive–compulsive disorder") and [substance use disorder (SUD)](/wiki/Substance_use_disorder "Substance use disorder"). They presented work to an American Association of Behavioral and Social Sciences conference, but suggested that much more research is needed before it could be proposed to the [APA](/wiki/American_Psychiatric_Association "American Psychiatric Association") that limerence be included in the [DSM](/wiki/Diagnostic_and_Statistical_Manual_of_Mental_Disorders "Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders"). They began conducting an unpublished study and reported to *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")* that about 25% or 30% of their participants had experienced a limerent relationship as they defined it.[[27]](#cite_note-usatoday-27) Wakin has stated that his concept involves people in [relationships](/wiki/Intimate_relationship "Intimate relationship"), where a person is obsessed with their partner to the detriment of the relationship, even to the point of a [breakup](/wiki/Breakup "Breakup").[[27]](#cite_note-usatoday-27)[[118]](#cite_note-potentgrip-118)[[320]](#cite_note-:24-320)

The concepts of limerence, [romantic love](/wiki/Romantic_love "Romantic love"), [passionate love](/wiki/Passionate_love "Passionate love") (and so on) have been compared to OCD since 1998, according to a theory invented by other authors.[[44]](#cite_note-fisher1998-44)[[246]](#cite_note-leckmanmayes-246)[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[138]](#cite_note-co-opted-138) This was partly based on a theoretical comparison between preoccupation features, like worries about a family member being harmed and a need for things to be "just right".[[246]](#cite_note-leckmanmayes-246)[[138]](#cite_note-co-opted-138) This is also sometimes paired with a theory involving the [neurotransmitter](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin"), but experimental evidence for that is ambiguous.[[44]](#cite_note-fisher1998-44)[[46]](#cite_note-fisher2002-46)[[6]](#cite_note-proximateandultimate-6)[[247]](#cite_note-:28-247) A 2025 study found no association between [SSRI](/wiki/Selective_serotonin_reuptake_inhibitor "Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor") use and obsessive thinking about a loved one or the intensity of romantic love.[[247]](#cite_note-:28-247) [Neuroscientist](/wiki/Neuroscientist "Neuroscientist") blogger Tom Bellamy has argued that limerence is distinct from OCD on the basis of psychological and neurobiological differences. OCD is characterized by compulsions to perform rituals that ease some type of fear, whereas limerence initially starts with a period of joy and only reaches a stage of anxiety when a pair bond cannot be formed.[[232]](#cite_note-bellamy-ocd-232)

[Helen Fisher](/wiki/Helen_Fisher_(anthropologist) "Helen Fisher (anthropologist)") has commented on Wakin & Vo in 2008, stating that limerence is romantic love and that "They are associating the negative aspects of it with the term, and that can be a disorder."[[27]](#cite_note-usatoday-27) Fisher is one of the original authors to compare limerence to OCD, and has proposed that romantic love is a "natural addiction" which can be either positive or negative depending on the situation.[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7)[[44]](#cite_note-fisher1998-44) Fisher stated again in 2024 that she does not think there is any difference between limerence and romantic love.[[117]](#cite_note-madlyinlove-117) [Sandra Langeslag](/wiki/Sandra_Langeslag "Sandra Langeslag") commented in 2026 that limerence is a form of passionate love or infatuation.[[313]](#cite_note-:5-313)

In 2017, Wakin has stated that he feels that brain scans of limerence would help establish it as "something unlike everything that has been diagnosed already",[[321]](#cite_note-:12-321) but brain scans have been described by Fisher's team since as far back as 2002.[[46]](#cite_note-fisher2002-46)[[27]](#cite_note-usatoday-27)[[2]](#cite_note-beam-limerence-fisher-2)[[322]](#cite_note-322) In Fisher et al.'s original brain scan experiments, all participants spent more than 85% of their waking hours thinking about their loved one.[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7) Wakin also claims that a person experiencing limerence can never be satiated, even if their feelings are reciprocated.[[321]](#cite_note-:12-321)[[320]](#cite_note-:24-320) Tennov found many cases of nonlimerent people who described their limerent partners being "stricken with a kind of insatiability", and that "no degree of attentiveness was ever sufficient".[[323]](#cite_note-323) According to Tennov's theory, the intensity of limerence diminishes with reciprocity, and it's prolonged inside a relationship when the LO behaves in a nonlimerent way.[[59]](#cite_note-conceptions-of-limerence-59)[[324]](#cite_note-324) Other mainstream authors have stated that obsession inside a relationship when it's a problem could be related to [self-esteem](/wiki/Self-esteem "Self-esteem") and an insecure [attachment style](/wiki/Attachment_style "Attachment style").[[33]](#cite_note-acevedo2009-33)[[325]](#cite_note-325)[[326]](#cite_note-326)[[300]](#cite_note-:110-300)

In the 1999 preface to her revised edition of *Love and Limerence*, [Dorothy Tennov](/wiki/Dorothy_Tennov "Dorothy Tennov") describes limerence as an aspect of basic [human nature](/wiki/Human_nature "Human nature") and remarks that "Reaction to limerence theory depends partly on acquaintance with the evidence for it and partly on personal experience. People who have not experienced limerence are baffled by descriptions of it and are often resistant to the evidence that it exists. To such outside observers, limerence seems pathological."[[157]](#cite_note-Tennov_1999_x-157) Tennov states that her studies suggest limerence is normal,[[20]](#cite_note-:40-20)[[327]](#cite_note-327)[[328]](#cite_note-328) that it's too often interpreted as "mental illness", and that even those who experienced limerence of a distressing variety were "fully functioning, rational, emotionally stable, normal, nonneurotic, nonpathological members of society", "characterized as responsible and quite sane". Tragedies such as [violence](/wiki/Violence "Violence"), she says, involve limerence when it's "augmented and distorted" by other conditions.[[329]](#cite_note-329)[[330]](#cite_note-330)

In a 2005 Q&A, Tennov was asked if limerence could ever lead to a situation like the movie *[Fatal Attraction](/wiki/Fatal_Attraction "Fatal Attraction")* (which has been called "[obsessive love](/wiki/Obsessive_love "Obsessive love")"), but Tennov replied that the movie seemed to depict a caricature.[[331]](#cite_note-331)[[332]](#cite_note-332) Limerence and [stalking](/wiki/Stalking "Stalking") are separate phenomena with different causes.[[25]](#cite_note-bellamy-stalking-25)[[23]](#cite_note-:43-23) Most romantic stalkers are an [ex-partner](/wiki/Breakup "Breakup"), [erotomanic](/wiki/Erotomania "Erotomania"), have a [personality disorder](/wiki/Personality_disorder "Personality disorder"), are [intellectually](/wiki/Intellect "Intellect") limited or [socially](/wiki/Social_skills "Social skills") incompetent.[[333]](#cite_note-333)[[7]](#cite_note-fisher2016-7) One writer who investigated the phenomenon of limerence videos on [TikTok](/wiki/TikTok "TikTok") in 2024 wrote that it seemed to her that the many videos created by the [relationship coaches](/wiki/Relationship_coaching "Relationship coaching") there were actually about [social media stalking](/wiki/Cyberstalking "Cyberstalking") rather than having anything at all to do with limerence.[[334]](#cite_note-:19-334)
